plainMeaning: This verse simply names a string of the living Jinas — Candrānana, Candrabāhu, Bhujanga, Īśvara, Nemiprabha, Vīrasena, Mahābhadra and Devayaśas — calling them all 'lord'.
recitation: caṁdāṇaṇo siricaṁda, bāhudevo bhujaṁga isarao | nemipaha vīraseṇo, mahabhaddo devajasa sāmī ||

wordByWord.gloss: Candrānana (the 'moon-faced' Jina); the 12th of the twenty living Jinas
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit caṁdāṇaṇa < Sanskrit candra ('moon') + ānana ('face') = 'moon-faced'; nominative -o.
wordByWord.notes: First of the eight viharamāṇa Jinas named in this verse, continuing the roll-call that the previous verse left at the eleventh.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit siri < Sanskrit śrī ('glory, splendour', honorific) + caṁda < candra ('moon'); here forming the front of the name read with the next word.
wordByWord.notes: Read together with bāhudevo as 'Śrī Candrabāhu-deva', the 13th living Jina; siri functions as a reverential prefix.

wordByWord.gloss: lord Bāhu (i.e. Candrabāhu); the 13th living Jina
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bāhu < Sanskrit bāhu ('arm') + deva ('lord, divine one'); nominative -o.
wordByWord.notes: With siricaṁda completes the name of viharamāṇa Jina no. 13, conventionally listed as Candrabāhu.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bhujaṁga < Sanskrit bhujaṅga ('serpent', lit. 'that which moves by bending').
wordByWord.notes: Standard lists give this Jina as Bhujangadeva; the verse uses the short stem.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit isara < Sanskrit īśvara ('lord, ruler', from √īś 'to govern'); nominative -o with hiatus-glide.

wordByWord.gloss: Nemiprabha ('radiance of the wheel-rim'); the 16th living Jina
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit nemi ('felly/rim of a wheel') + paha < Sanskrit prabhā ('radiance, light').
wordByWord.notes: A distinct Jina from the Tīrthaṅkara Nemi(nātha) of our own region; not to be conflated.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vīra ('hero, valiant') + seṇa < Sanskrit senā ('army'); nominative -o.
Prakrit maha < Sanskrit mahā ('great') + bhadda < bhadra ('auspicious, good'); nominative -o.

wordByWord.gloss: Devayaśas ('glory of the gods'); the 19th living Jina
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit deva ('god') + jasa < Sanskrit yaśas ('glory, fame').

wordByWord.notes: An honorific governing the whole list — 'these lords' — closing the line; in chanting it attaches especially to Devayaśas (Devajasa-sāmī) but reverentially crowns all the names enumerated.

literalTranslation: Candrānana, the glorious Candrabāhu-lord, Bhujanga, Īśvara; Nemiprabha, Vīrasena, Mahābhadra, Devayaśas — [these] lords.
idiomaticTranslation: Candrānana and the glorious lord Candrabāhu, Bhujanga and Īśvara; Nemiprabha, Vīrasena, Mahābhadra and Devayaśas — all of them sovereign lords among the living Jinas.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This is a pure name-litany, and its devotional force lies precisely in that bareness: the reciter does nothing but pronounce, one after another, the personal names of eight currently-teaching Jinas. Picking up the count exactly where the previous verse stopped — at the eleventh, Vajradhara — it supplies numbers twelve through nineteen: Candrānana, Candrabāhu (here dignified as siri-...-bāhudevo, 'the glorious lord'), Bhujanga, Īśvara, Nemiprabha, Vīrasena, Mahābhadra and Devayaśas. The single honorific sāmī ('lord') at the line's end is not attached to one Jina but crowns the entire series, so the verse reads as a breathless list of 'lords'. The following verse will add the twentieth, Ajitavīrya, and then gather all of them into the explicit salute 'I bow to the twenty Jinendras'. So this verse is structurally a hinge: it carries no verb of homage of its own, holding the act of bowing in suspension across the three-verse unit until it is discharged in the next verse. Note that Nemiprabha must not be confused with our own region's Nemi(nātha): the names here belong to Jinas of the Mahāvideha lands, distinct persons who happen to share auspicious name-elements (candra, nemi, vīra) with figures known from Bharata. The poet's restraint — eight names, one honorific, no embellishment — mirrors the doctrinal point that these Jinas are simply, presently, there to be named and revered.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain teaching that, while Bharata's time-cycle is now in its declining fifth ara where no Tīrthaṅkara is born, the Mahāvideha lands remain perpetually in a flourishing era, so that exactly twenty Jinas are alive and teaching at this very moment — four in each of the five Mahāvideha regions. To name them is to affirm that omniscient guidance is never wholly absent from the universe even when absent from our own land; the worshipper who cannot meet a Jina here can still revere a Jina who exists now. Naming each individually also honours the principle that each Jina is a distinct soul who attained Jinahood by his own effort, not an interchangeable abstraction.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Chanted slowly, this verse becomes a small act of presence: the reciter calls eight living teachers by name and thereby cultivates the conviction that the path is open and attended even today. It pairs naturally with devotion to Sīmandhara, the best-known of these twenty, and steadies the mind for the consolidated salutation that follows.

crossReferences.text: Vīs Vihamāṇ-jina salutation tradition (twenty living Jinas of Mahāvideha)
crossReferences.source: Śvetāmbara devotional usage; cf. Simandhar Swami entry, Wikipedia

crossReferences.text: Following verse (ashtottari-tirthmala/39): 'iya ee saṁpayaṁ viharamāṇe, vande vīsa jiṇaṁde' — 'thus I bow to these twenty presently-wandering Jinendras'
crossReferences.source: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha ed.)

sources.title: Simandhar Swami — the twenty living Tīrthaṅkaras of Mahāvideha (4 × 5 regions = 20)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming the doctrine of twenty currently-living viharamāṇa Jinas and the perpetual fourth-ara conditions of Mahāvideha

sources.title: Mahā-videha — Jainpedia Glossary
sources.consultedFor: The 32 vijayas of Mahāvideha and the count of living Jinas resident there

sources.title: Jain Cosmography [5] — HereNow4U
sources.consultedFor: Cosmographic placement of the Mahāvideha jinālayas and the present Tīrthaṅkaras

notes: The verse is a bare name-list of viharamāṇa (living Mahāvideha) Jinas; identification with the standard 20-Jina sequence (nos. 12–19 here: Candrānana, Candrabāhu, Bhujanga, Īśvara, Nemiprabha, Vīrasena, Mahābhadra, Devayaśas) is secure because the preceding verse closes at the eleventh (Vajradhara) and the following verse adds the twentieth (Ajitavīrya) and the explicit 'twenty Jinendras' salute. The chief ambiguity is the segmentation 'siricaṁda bāhudevo': read here as a single name 'Śrī Candrabāhu-deva' (Jina no. 13), with siri as honorific prefix and -deva as honorific suffix; some readers might parse 'Śrīcandra' and 'Bāhudeva' as two names, but that would yield nine names and disturb the 12–19 count, so the unified reading is preferred. 'Nemiprabha' is a Mahāvideha Jina, NOT Tīrthaṅkara Neminātha of Bharata. Orthography (e.g. ઇસરઓ, મહભદ્દો, દેવજસ) is preserved verbatim from the book and not normalised. No verified Prakrit-dictionary URL for the individual proper names was located, so glosses rest on transparent Sanskrit etymology; exact name-forms may vary slightly across editions and sects.
